content:
Yep, all of this is valid. Honestly I realize that in these cases I should always provide the direct translation (as clear as possible), as a sort of "intermediary" for other languages to iterate from, before myself (or whoever-self) iterating from it into prosaic english.

I'll keep this in mind for future projects. Thanks!

I'll clarify the main file to read closer to the original "and eventually, forget it completely" and then figure out our choice of English wording when I have more braincells later 😵‍💫

然后将自己彻底遗忘 - 
For this phrase, should it be something like "and then forgets them utterly"?
my chinese is only middling I'm afraid 😅
that's why I called for someone to help~
you might get a quicker response in <#1402941858606219294> though, unless this is a question *specifically* regarding the english version of the game.
alright, I'll copy it over
although, reading over this line again in both languages I do actually think "and then forgets them utterly" is a more poetic tl in line with the chinese, but it's a fairly minor nitpick

```The crumbling idol gazes upon its people.
It watches them survive, multiply, and fight; only to eventually forget it.
The crumbling idol longs for its people.```
Assuming this is the line we're talking about, I'm pretty sure "utterly" *was* in the original EN we started with, and this was a decision on Lens' part. lemme check!

where does this line come from?
Should be something like “and then forgets itself/himself/herself throughly”, but I guess the environment of this sentence  affects its accurate meaning here

Here's the original, before Lens put up his TL. Not "utterly" but "entirely"
```The crumbling idol gazes upon its people.
Watches them survive, multiply, and fight, only to eventually forget it entirely.
The crumbling idol longs for its people.```
What do you think, Flarin? Here's the original CN:
```坍塌的偶像注视着自己的子民。
看他们生存、繁衍、互相争斗，然后将自己彻底遗忘。
坍塌的偶像怀念着自己的子民。```
specifically, this is the flavor text for Phantasmal Dive, the one with the big statue background. I can pull up the picture if you want

Ah it’s okay 
so here “自己” means “the crumbling idol”…
oh, interesting. I see it in that middle line too now. we probably wouldn't repeat it like that in English though

for me it could be something like “it watches them then throw it in entire oblivion”…
the problem is the first half of the sentence has subject “it”(the idol) while the second part has the subject “them” (it’s people), so some alternation of the sentence’s structure is needed here imo
interesting.

how about 
`It watches them survive, multiply, fight... and eventually, throw it entirely into oblivion.`
that actually slaps way harder
this works for me
dope.
silly question, but would "beholds" work in place of "gazes upon" here?

```The crumbling idol beholds its people.
It watches them survive, multiply, fight——and eventually, throw it entirely into oblivion.
The crumbling idol longs for its people...```

sure
“beholds” sounds better as some “abstract” gaze xD
I like to pretend I'm good at prose
I'll update the main sheet and <#1416615133479960647> as well!
oh wait, so it's the *people* who are forgetting the idol?
I got the subject and object totally reversed
Well actually, huh. I guess it could go either way
It then would read that the people threw *themselves* into oblivion, which... Still makes the last line make sense
because the first past of the sentence is "it watches them survive, breed, fight, then [the part I was asking about]"
so I thought it was "and then [the idol] forgets them [the people] utterly/completely" but if it's the opposite then that changes the whole phrase

I'm guessing 将 here is the same as 把? so the phrase uses the passive sentence structure and means basically "...and then [the people] make/cause it [the idol] to be thoroughly/completely forgotten" I think?

The trick here is the subject (idol) is omitted in the beginning for CN text 
so it’s actually a clause “(the idol) watches (them survive,… , throw it into oblivion)”
And since the next sentence mentions the idol 怀念 (miss/long for something used to have) its people, probably it is the idol that got forgotten

so to be clear, the poem is meant to be read:
"the idol watches (the idol's) people throw the idol into oblivion; the idol longs for (the idol's) people (because the people have forgotten the idol)"
and not:
"the idol watches (the idol's) people throw themselves/each other into oblivion; the idol longs for (the idol's) people (because the people are now gone)"
yeppp

I didn't get the phrase "throw into oblivion" exactly, but if I'm understanding it correctly then yea, the idol watches its people survive, etc.. and then completely forget [the idol], which is why it now longs for its people (who have forgotten it)
I would actually love it if someone could break down the last phrase in the second sentence haha, starting from 然后 so I can get a better understanding of the structure 😅
yes, the idol watches the people eventually forget about it (the idol) completely
the problem is that the phrase "eventually forget it entirely" just sounds terrible and i spent like ten minutes trying to figure out how to make it not sound terrible before giving up
"throw" is a weird verb here though, not sure I like it
forgetting is such a passive action and throw is a very strongly active word
but yeah I never could figure out a way to word it so anything different works

> 看他们生存、繁衍、互相争斗，然后将自己彻底遗忘。
```
看 - watch
他们 - them
生存、 - exist/survive (used in this context as a verb, so it would be survive)
繁衍、 - reproduce
互相争斗，- 互相 = between each other, 争斗 = fight > "fight each other"

[ 然后将自己彻底遗忘。]
然后 - and then
将 - a preposition, this basically "marks" the subject? not sure what the specific grammatical term is for this
自己 - oneself, in this case "itself" as it's being used in reference to the idol
彻底遗忘 - 彻底 = completely, 遗忘 = to forget > "to completely/utterly forget"
```
the key things when it comes to translating the latter section of this sentence is that there are effectively 3 components that comprise this phrase:
1. the passage of time > 然后 explicitly marks a temporal distinction, meaning this is something that happens "after" the events prior
2. the intensity > 彻底 is a very emphatic word which stresses the extent of what is happening, meaning that the forgetting is being done "completely"
3. the forgetting > 遗忘, the verb, which is of course the act itself of forgetting which is occurring in the statement

all 3 components must be included in order to properly convey the exact same tone and concept of the original
the problem is that I was unable to incorporate all three - "eventually" covers 1, "forget" covers 2, and "entirely" covers 3, but the phrase "eventually forget it entirely" sounds terribly awkward and stiff in English which fails to work well in a prosaic fashion
as such, I ended up compromising with an incomplete translation by incorporating only 1 and 2, and concluding that the act of forgetting already implies that it was done in completion, and figured the prosaic value in a more natural English structure made up for the cost of not explicitly specifying "completely" in the text
Does "and then forget it utterly" not work for a tl?
I feel like utterly is a more... artistic flavor for 'completely'
it works in the sense that it does convey the exact meaning, but it's still a very awkward phrase
Actually what makes it hard is 将because I'm pretty sure it's like a passive grammatical marker
So that last phrase actually reverses the normal structure of the last two phrases
So it's kind of like... "it watches them survive, etc... and then is itself forgotten thoroughly/utterly/completely" ?

the problem is that in english the phrase "forget completely" is generally only used in an active sense where the subject immediate predicates it, but this structure is more of a passive structure where the idol is being forgotten
To preserve the structure of the last phrase

from a writing point of view this is really awkward and would only work if it was worded as "to be utterly forgotten", or "to be forgotten completely", but now you're changing the sentence considerably because you've converted it into passive voice which is not the case for the original
Am I wrong in thinking 将 is similar to 把 here?
Like it functions the same?
将 is the literary/fancy form of 把, yeah
it's like 之 vs 的
Yeah so in that case starting from 然后 the phrase has shifted to passive form
hmm
So I do think "It watches them survive, breed, fight each other, and then is itself utterly forgotten."
passive would be more like 被
That's a good point
将 isn't passive voice, it just signifies the object that the action is happening to
there's like a specific word for this kind of grammatical construct but I forget what it is
https://laoshi.io/grammar/en/354/

content: Hmm... I feel like I learnt it at some point but it's out of reach in my mind lol
preposition, right...I literally said that exact word like five minutes ago so not sure how it slipped my mind lol
but yeah the existence of 将 doesn't necessarily signify passive voice, it's active (you can see the examples on that page are all active)
in this case (at least how I read it), it's very clearly active voice because the thing that is doing all of these verbs is the people
Yeah, that's good to know, TIL

the people survive
the people multiply
the people, eventually, forget the existence of their god
either way this is just one of those things that's just not possible to TL 100% because the structure of the phrase just doesn't work in English
so some level of compromise must be made, it's just deciding at what point you want to compromise, and how
I guess there's no need to preserve the structure
structure is one of those things that you want to preserve if possible, but sometimes just isn't because languages work differently

on further consideration, I don't think I agree with this because although it sounds much fancier, the verb "throw" is too ambiguous in its meaning.

Aiming for poeticism is good, but only insofar that the fundamental meaning of the text is not obscured, which the verb "throw" does, because "throw" is simply not a word commonly used in the context of forgetting.

The issue here is that the phrase "throw into oblivion" is just not a clear phrase in terms of what it means, whereas the verb "to forget" is extremely clear and would not be confused in any way by anyone. I would wager that if you asked someone, without context, what the sentence "The people throw the crumbling idol into oblivion" means, a proportion significantly less than 100% would say that it means the people forgot the idol. Conversely, if you were to ask someone, without context, what the sentence "The people forget the crumbling idol" means, I guarantee you that 100% of them would say it means the people forgot the idol (since, obviously, that is literally exactly what it says).

The word "throw" in this context implies a much different context, and the following text of "into oblivion" is not a particular intuitive followup. The wording here will likely cause misinterpretation of the meaning - are the people overthrowing the idol? do they actively demolish it? to "throw into oblivion" sounds like they are destroying it and actively ridding themselves of it, which is an extremely different action from passively forgetting about something over time. Even if a significant majority of readers interpret it in the intended meaning (being slowly forgotten over time), that majority is not going to be 100% of readers because some will interpret it differently due to the ambiguity of the word
quite literally my exact case in point here - the very first reaction to this text after it was changed is "huh I don't quite get what 'throw into oblivion' means"

again (not to beat a dead horse since I've said this a few times), but "fancifying" a text is a very complex process which needs to be done very carefully; using fanciful words without being *very careful* about how it fits into context is a problem because you run a significant risk of obscuring the original meaning. Fancier phrasing does sound nice, but the inherent problem with using more prosaic words is that it's usually more difficult to understand which comes with an implicit risk of detracting or modifying from the original meaning.

Every time something is changed, you need to take a step back and properly re-evaluate it in context to judge whether or not that change works. The phrase "throw into oblivion" is fire, yes, but after your initial response of "damn that's cool", you have to then step back and reconsider whether or not it fits properly in context. This is where most people who try to edit/proofread their own work have issues (and also why as a general rule, translators cannot be their own editors unless they are *very* skilled), because it's extremely difficult to detach your own biases and contexts from influencing your judgement of the result. Any rewordings you consider will be influenced by your understanding of the original text, and that knowledge will bias your judgement of any subsequent phrases. However, people who end up reading that result will be judging it in a void with no context, because they're not going to know what the original was or what the process was for editing the original text.

*We* all know that "throw into oblivion" in this context means to forget, because we literally came up with it as an alternative to the word "forget", but readers won't have that context - what proportion of readers are going to see "throw into oblivion" and assume uprising, rebellion, or destruction? Even if that proportion is small, say, 10-20%, is it worth an extremely minor improvement for the 80% over a *total loss of meaning* for the 20%?

This is also why I have been emphasizing multiple-pass changes of text, because one of the common tricks when it comes to one-man translation is for the translator to translate first, put it aside, and then come back to edit it after a significant amount of time has passed because by then they will have lost immediate context for their work, and it's much easier to work on editing text in a void because that is how the reader will parse it. The same principle applies for writing as well, and you can find dozens if not hundreds of articles espousing this exact process because it's a proven method and it *works*

one ten-second google search:
<https://aprildavila.com/self-editing/>
<https://medium.com/@karenhoughwrites/edit-with-fresh-eyes-05a8dd2e28f8>
<https://kmallan.com/2017/12/01/editing-hacks-how-to-read-your-work-with-fresh-eyes/>
<https://stephaniewrobel.substack.com/p/how-i-self-edit>

also assuming that the intent is to preserve the word "oblivion" being used rather than the verb "forget", some verbs that would fit better would be:
- fade away into oblivion (issue: the subject here would be the idol, rather than the people)
- drift into oblivion
- swallowed by oblivion
- crumbled into oblivion
- lost to oblivion

again the biggest issue here is just the english structure since the people are the subject and the idol is the object, and preserving that is very difficult because the structure of english just means it's not very common to have B come before A in the context of "A forgets B" without it being written in passive voice. 

the only phrase in natural english that I can think of which is an active forgetting and includes the word "oblivion" would be "consign to oblivion", but even then there's a very clear implication that it's deliberate and intentional which doesn't quite fit

some compromise will need to be made for the english translation of this line either way, it’s just a choice of what to give up…

- if we give up on preserving the structure, it’s quite easy to simply split it into two sentences but it will read very differently 
- if we give up on preserving the grammatical accuracy, we can convert the verbiage from active to passive voice which makes it quite easy (the idol is forgotten completely, or its memory fades away into oblivion)
- if we give up on keeping all aspects of the original text, we can drop either “eventually” or “completely” to have it work better in english (this was my original choice)
- if we give up on having it flow/read well in natural english, we can keep the literal wording though it will sound a bit awkward
“throw into oblivion”, to me, is basically the last choice except the wording chosen is now obscure/cryptic instead of awkward so nothing has really changed

This is anecdotal, but it is a good proof of concept and I invite you to do the same: I provided this exact text, as-is, to a few of my friends with no context and asked them to explain it back to me in their own words as to what they thought was happening

- out of the 4 I asked, all 4 of them understood the general idea (a sentient god watches over his people, sees the rise of their civilization, and then longs for the past once he is abandoned)
- 1 of them interpreted "thrown into oblivion" to mean that the people "threw off" the "shackles of religion", meaning they deliberately did away with idol worship as part of the advancement of their civilization
- 2 of them interpreted "thrown into oblivion" to mean that they gave up their faith to follow a different god - one thought the idol was "thrown away" to join a new religion, the other thought the idol was "given up" for a different one
- 1 of them interpreted "thrown into oblivion" as "left behind in the past" - this is would be the correct interpretation, although he did not use the word "forgotten" but just assumed the idol was left behind as the civilization advanced

After they provided me with their own interpretations, I asked them if they believed that text could be interpreted as the idol being forgotten over time as the people moved on. All four of them agreed that that it would be a valid interpretation, but none of them came to that conclusion on their first exposure to the text. As such, had I not deliberately prompted them with that interpretation, they would not have thought of it themselves

Again, this is fairly anecdotal evidence but I think if you were to repeat the experiment, you would not see a 100% consistency in the interpretation of the second line, which demonstrates that it has failed at serving its purpose of being an accurate translation

Also as an additional yet also vitally important note - remember that the whole purpose of this particular translation is to serve as an accurate translated English base by which other language translators can use to translate into their language when their team does not have a direct CN translator

Given that, consider how someone would translate the phrase "throw it entirely into oblivion" into a different language when seeing it in English. Do you think those translators, not having read our convo or having any understanding of the original Chinese text, would translate that phrase properly into "forgotten" as per the original text? Or do you think those translators would translate that verb into "to throw", as explicitly written in English? Does this phrase truly serve to preserve the original meaning, or are you amplifying a loss in meaning via this change in wording?
reading through the professional translation and overall it's heads and shoulders higher quality than the MTL was, but looks like a lot of the suggested terms were declined
they went with Brilpert for the town name (which is fine, the problem is the half dozen other names it got translated to elsewhere in the game)

they went with "the Coming" instead of the ritual of descent which I guess is a narrative choice but someone in the story discussion channel on discord explicitly called out "the Coming" as a very confusing term, which I agree with
overall it's much better, I like this a lot
pretty optimistic for the full a1 translation

there's a few hiccups (doll is NOT the kind of person to say "good nappin'?"), but those are pretty natural because I think that's just a byproduct of working on the script directly without getting the ingame context for certain lines/situations

will need to ask Ace if we're allowed to proofread the a1 script afterwards just to fix minor grammar/wording issues like that, but if we are it should be a fairly quick job doable within a day or two

there is still a very obvious strong chatGPT influence in certain parts but...as someone who has a bit of incidental interaction with professional translators on a fairly regular basis, that's kind of just how the industry is nowadays so there's not much helping it
this is extremely obvious chatGPT output due to the structure but I think unless you have a lot of experience working with LLM text, it's not something a normal person recognizes

that's really, really rough... I'm actually really concerned.
announcing to the world that the story has been fully human-translated, and then having players discover ChatGPT was used is a bed you can NOT un-shit. The reputation of Morimens in the west is at risk and I really hope BIAV is aware...
